summary: Joel announces that his blog has migrated to a new server hosted inside Fog Creek's offices. The migration process revealed numerous hardcoded URLs throughout the Manila content management system, requiring manual fixes. He notes the irony that while the site looks identical to readers, significant behind-the-scenes work was needed to make it portable. The post highlights the practical challenges of moving a web application between servers when URLs are embedded throughout the codebase.
key_points: Joel migrated his blog to a self-hosted server inside Fog Creek's offices, moving away from external hosting
The Manila CMS had hardcoded URLs scattered throughout, making the migration tedious and error-prone
He emphasizes the importance of making sites portable by eliminating hardcoded URLs
Static assets were still pointing to UserLand's servers and needed manual adjustment
money_quotes: I know, it looks the same to you … but this version of Joel On Software is actually running on a new server inside the Fog Creek offices.
I've been having a lot of fun (!) discovering all the places in Manila where URLs are hardcoded.
I'd like to get rid of as many hardcoded URLs as possible to make sure the site stays portable.
key_insight: Hardcoded URLs throughout a web application create hidden migration costs that only become apparent when you try to move the system to a new server.
